---
title: "{{< var icon.computer-activity >}} Vibecoding: Tic-Tac-Toe"
subtitle: "Discussion 2, {{< var course.code >}}, {{< var course.term >}}"
---

Build a small app with a coding agent, then investigate what the agent built.

**Total time:** 20--30 minutes

## {{< var icon.task >}} Steps

### Task 1: Build Tic-Tac-Toe

**Time:** 3--5 minutes

Use your AI tool to build a two-player tic-tac-toe game.
Describe what you want and let the tool do the work.

Your game should:

-   [ ] Show a 3-by-3 board.
-   [ ] Let two players take turns as X and O.
-   [ ] Detect when someone wins or the game is a draw.

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## TA note

Circulate and make sure everyone has their AI tool open and is getting output.
This task should be very fast.
If someone has been stuck for more than a minute or two, check their setup first.
:::

### Task 2: Add a Computer Opponent

**Time:** 3--5 minutes

Add a single-player mode where you play against the computer.

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## TA note

Do not steer students toward any particular approach.
Let the AI decide how to implement the computer player.
Some students may get minimax, while others may get something simpler.
That variation is useful for Task 3.
:::

### Task 3: Investigate the Computer

**Time:** 10 minutes

Now that you have a computer opponent, think about whether it is doing its job correctly.

**Step 1: Test it.**
A correct agent should never lose — it either wins or draws.
Play several games and try to beat it.

-   [ ] Can you beat it?
-   [ ] Does it always block you when you are about to win?
-   [ ] Does it always take a winning move when it has one?
-   [ ] Does it play the same move in the same board position every time?

**Step 2: Find out what it's doing.**
Ask the AI what algorithm the computer player uses and how it works.

-   [ ] What does the AI say it built?
-   [ ] Does that match what you observed in Step 1?

**Step 3: Verify it.**
Now that you have a claim about what the agent is doing, how do you check whether it is actually doing that?
Come up with at least two ways to test the claim, then try them.

Some directions to consider: adding displays or logging to make the computer's process visible, asking the AI questions, constructing specific board positions for testing.

**Key question:** How confident are you that your approaches tell you what the agent is actually doing?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## TA note

The AI usually produces minimax but may produce a rules-based agent.
Either way, the point of this task is not to identify the algorithm — it is to get students to interrogate whether it is correct and how they could know.

**On behavioral testing:** Outcome alone is a weak signal.
A buggy agent might still beat most students most of the time.
Push them toward making the process visible rather than just playing more games.

**On visualizations:** There is uncertainty over whether the visualization accurately reflects the computer's process. A label that says "I chose this move because it leads to a win in 3 moves" could reflect the actual minimax computation — or it could be a post-hoc string generated independently of the code that actually chose the move.
Seeing scores next to moves is stronger evidence, but even that could be fabricated.
The only way to be certain is to read the code.

Push back on confident answers:

-   How do you know the visualization is showing you what is really happening?
-   How do you know the AI's explanation is accurate?
-   Could the label be generated after the fact, independent of what the code actually does?
:::

### Optional Task 4: Keep Building

Done early?
Pick one of two directions.

**Option A: Start fresh.**
Start a brand new conversation with your AI tool and build tic-tac-toe again from scratch.
Does the AI make the same design choices?
Does the computer player use the same algorithm?
What is different, and what is the same?

**Option B: Keep building.**
Add something to your existing game:

-   [ ] Difficulty levels, such as easy, medium, and hard.
-   [ ] Nicer visuals, such as styling, colours, or animations.
-   [ ] A score tracker across multiple games.
-   [ ] An undo button.

Whatever you do, keep asking yourself: **How do you know it is doing what you think it is doing?**

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## TA note

For Option A, prompt students to compare their two versions.
Did they get the same algorithm both times?
If not, why might that be?
This is a good entry point into questions about AI consistency and determinism.

For Option B, the reflective question is the point.
Encourage students to notice when they feel confident versus uncertain about whether the AI did what they asked.
:::

## Class Discussion

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## TA note

Aim for 5--10 minutes.
The questions below are roughly in order.
The first two are warm-up questions, the third is where the discussion gets more interesting, and the fourth is the closer.
:::

### What algorithm did the AI build for you?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## TA note

Take a quick poll: who got minimax, who got a rules-based agent, who got something else?
Then ask one or two students to describe how they figured it out.
This surfaces the different investigation strategies from Task 3.
:::

### How did you try to verify it?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## TA note

Students may have tried playing games, asking the AI, adding a visualization, or reading the code.
Draw out the limitations of each approach with these questions:

-   If you asked the AI to explain itself, how do you know the explanation is accurate?
-   If you added a visualization, how do you know the visualization is showing what the code actually does?

Important point here: **the only way to know for sure is to read the code.**
:::

### Can you be confident in what the AI built, even if you cannot read the code?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## TA note

Students can observe behavior and run tests, but they can never be certain without reading the code.
Most of the time, they will not be able to read the code --- not because it is hidden, but because they do not yet know how.
This applies to software built by humans too, not just AI.
:::

### How far are you willing to trust software you cannot verify?

Consider these scenarios in order:

-   A game
-   A messaging app
-   A grades system
-   Medical software
-   Government or military systems

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## TA note

There is no single right answer.
The goal is for students to recognize that this tradeoff exists and that they will encounter it throughout their lives.
Somewhere on that spectrum, they would want more than behavioral evidence.
:::

## {{< var icon.resource >}} Resources
